title: Global Youth Mental Health Research Fellowship 2026: Funded Program for Emerging Leaders
summary: The Global Youth Mental Health Research Fellowship 2026 is a fully funded 12-month opportunity designed to support emerging leaders in child and adolescent mental health from Pakistan, Indonesia, and Colombia. This prestigious fellowship provides structured training, hands-on research experience, mentorship, and global collaboration opportunities for early-career professionals and individuals with lived experience who are committed to improving youth mental health outcomes. About the Program The Global Youth Mental Health Research Fellowship is implemented by the Global Institute of Human Development (GIHD) at Shifa Tameer-e-Millat University in collaboration with leading global institutions, including The George Washington University, Universitas Gadjah Mada, Universidad de los Andes, and The Global Health Network. The program aims to build the next generation of leaders who can address mental health challenges among young people through research, advocacy, and evidence-based practice. This 12-month, full-time fellowship runs from 1 June 2026 to 30 May 2027. Fellows will be actively engaged in research projects under expert supervision, contributing to meaningful studies that inform policy and practice in global mental health. The program combines academic rigor with practical application, ensuring that participants gain both theoretical knowledge and real-world experience. Participants will also benefit from a structured curriculum covering key areas such as global mental health foundations, research design, ethics and safeguarding, randomized controlled trials, data analysis, and dissemination of findings. Through this comprehensive approach, the fellowship equips participants with the tools needed to drive impactful change in their communities. Why This Opportunity Matters Youth mental health is a growing global priority, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where access to services and research capacity may be limited. This fellowship directly addresses these gaps by investing in local talent and strengthening research capabilities in key regions. By focusing on early-career professionals and individuals with lived experience, the program ensures that diverse perspectives are included in shaping mental health solutions. This inclusive approach not only enhances the relevance of research but also promotes more effective and culturally responsive interventions. The fellowship also fosters international collaboration, connecting participants with global experts and networks. This exposure helps fellows understand broader trends in mental health while contributing locally grounded insights to the global discourse. What Participants Will Gain Participants in the fellowship will gain a combination of academic, professional, and personal development benefits. The program offers financial support through a fellowship stipend, along with coverage for research-related expenses and travel, allowing fellows to focus fully on their work. Fellows will develop advanced research skills through hands-on experience, working as part of multidisciplinary teams on ongoing projects. This practical exposure is complemented by mentorship from leading experts in global mental health, providing guidance on both technical and career development. In addition, participants will receive training in grant writing and scientific publication, enhancing their ability to secure funding and disseminate their work effectively. The fellowship also provides access to a global network through The Global Health Network, enabling collaboration with professionals across more than 180 countries. Upon successful completion, fellows will receive a certificate from the Global Mental Health Equity Lab at The George Washington University, adding significant value to their professional credentials and future career opportunities. Who Can Apply The fellowship is open to a diverse group of applicants who are committed to improving youth mental health. Eligible candidates include early-career psychiatrists, psychologists, academics in mental health, allied health professionals, and individuals working with young people in health or education settings. Importantly, the program also welcomes young people with lived experience of mental health challenges, recognizing the value of lived perspectives in shaping research and advocacy efforts. Applicants must have completed at least 16 years of education and/or have no more than five years of work experience in the mental health field. They must also be available to commit full-time for the entire duration of the fellowship. Eligible Areas Applicants must be based in Pakistan, Indonesia, or Colombia at the time of application. Application Process and Deadline Interested candidates must complete the online application form and submit all required materials before the deadline. The selection process includes shortlisting, interviews, and final evaluation. The application deadline is 11 May 2026 at 5 pm local time . Shortlisted candidates will be notified on 18 May 2026, with interviews scheduled shortly after. Final decisions will be communicated by 29 May 2026, and the fellowship will commence on 1 June 2026. Learn more and apply on the Global Health Network website . Explore More Opportunities for Youth Want to discover more fellowships, scholarships, internships, and global programs like this one? Visit Opportunities for Youth to explore more opportunities. The post Global Youth Mental Health Research Fellowship 2026: Funded Program for Emerging Leaders appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Code for Africa AI for Good Fellowship 2026: Paid Ethical AI Opportunity for African Technologists
summary: The Code for Africa AI for Good Fellowship 2026 offers a unique opportunity for technologists across Africa to apply their skills to real-world challenges while earning a stipend and building impactful solutions. Designed for mid-career professionals with experience in artificial intelligence and data science, this four-month fellowship supports the development of ethical, scalable AI tools that address pressing societal issues across the Sahel and neighbouring regions. About the Program The AI for Good Fellowship, implemented by Code for Africa (CfA) and supported by the Digitalise Youth consortium, is a fully funded program aimed at bridging the gap between technology and human rights work. The fellowship places selected participants in collaboration with Human Rights Defender (HRD) organisations, enabling them to co-create AI-driven solutions that strengthen civic engagement, counter misinformation, and support vulnerable communities. Over the course of four months, fellows will design and prototype tools such as multilingual AI assistants, systems for tracking harmful narratives, and data-driven solutions that inform advocacy and policy decisions. Participants will also receive mentorship from CfA’s TechLab, DataLab, and AI Sandbox, as well as guidance from global experts in AI, policy, and civil society. The program is highly practical, emphasizing real-world application and collaboration. Fellows will work directly with organisations operating in complex environments, ensuring that the solutions developed are context-sensitive, ethical, and scalable. Why This Opportunity Matters As artificial intelligence continues to shape societies globally, there is an increasing need for ethical and inclusive approaches to its development and deployment. This fellowship addresses that need by empowering African technologists to lead innovation in a way that prioritizes human rights, transparency, and social impact. The Sahel region faces significant challenges, including disinformation, climate vulnerability, and restricted civic space. By equipping technologists with the tools and partnerships needed to address these issues, the fellowship contributes to building resilient digital ecosystems and strengthening democratic participation. Importantly, the program also amplifies local expertise. By prioritizing applicants with strong ties to African communities, it ensures that solutions are grounded in lived realities rather than external assumptions. What Participants Will Gain Participants in the fellowship will benefit from both professional development and meaningful impact. The program offers hands-on experience in building AI solutions that respond to real challenges, allowing fellows to strengthen their technical and problem-solving skills. Fellows will also gain access to mentorship from leading experts and institutions, helping them refine their approaches to ethical AI and responsible innovation. The collaborative environment fosters connections with policymakers, civil society organisations, and fellow technologists, expanding professional networks across the continent. In addition, participants receive a monthly stipend of $500, providing financial support while they focus on their projects. By the end of the program, fellows will have developed tangible outputs, including prototypes, research briefs, and scalable implementation plans—valuable assets for career advancement. Who Can Apply The fellowship is open to mid-career technologists, including AI engineers, data scientists, and social innovators, who have demonstrated experience in using technology to address societal challenges. Applicants should have strong technical skills in areas such as machine learning, natural language processing, or data analysis, as well as experience working with real-world datasets. A commitment to ethical AI practices, human rights, and inclusive design is essential. Candidates must also demonstrate the ability to collaborate with diverse stakeholders, including civil society organisations and policymakers. Fluency in English, French, or Arabic is required, and familiarity with open-source tools or multilingual environments is considered an advantage. Eligible Areas Applicants must be based in one of the following countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Chad, Ethiopia, Guinea, Mali, Mauritania, Niger, Senegal, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, or Togo. Application Process and Deadline Interested candidates must complete the online application form and submit the required information demonstrating their experience, technical expertise, and motivation to participate in the fellowship. The application deadline is 11 May 2026 , and early submission is encouraged. Selected fellows will join a cohort of innovators working at the intersection of AI and social impact, with opportunities to showcase their work to regional and global partners. Learn more and apply on the official call for application . Explore More Opportunities for Youth Want to discover more fellowships, scholarships, internships, and global programs like this one? Visit Opportunities for Youth to explore more opportunities. title: Join Student Energy Guided Projects Cohort 3 (Solar Stream): Build Real Solar Solutions and Transform Your Community
summary: The Student Energy Guided Projects Cohort 3 (Solar Stream) is now open for applications, offering an incredible opportunity for youth-led teams to gain hands-on experience in the clean energy sector while making a meaningful impact in their communities. This program is designed to empower young innovators with the tools, mentorship, and funding needed to design and implement real-world solar photovoltaic (PV) projects. If your team is passionate about sustainability, climate action, and renewable energy, this is your chance to turn ideas into impactful solutions. About the Guided Projects Program The Student Energy Guided Projects initiative equips young people with critical early-career experience in the energy industry. Through this program, participants go beyond theory and actively engage in solving real community energy challenges. Under the Solar Stream, selected teams will: Identify pressing energy challenges within their communities Design practical solar photovoltaic (PV) systems Plan and execute installation projects Deliver sustainable, community-driven energy solutions This immersive experience ensures participants develop both technical and professional skills that are essential for thriving in the global clean energy transition. Why You Should Apply 1. Hands-On Project Experience Participants will gain real-world exposure by working through the entire project lifecycle, including: Feasibility studies System design and modeling Budgeting and financial planning Installation and commissioning This practical learning approach bridges the gap between academic knowledge and industry application. 2. Mentorship, Training Funding Selected teams will benefit from: Structured training modules Guidance from industry experts and mentors Access to funding of up to $15,000 to support project implementation This support ensures that teams are well-equipped to execute impactful and scalable energy solutions. 3. Create Lasting Community Impact Participants will: Deliver real solar energy installations Reduce energy costs and carbon emissions Contribute to sustainable development in their communities The projects are not just educational—they create measurable, long-term benefits. Team-Based Application Requirement Applications must be submitted as a team. Individual applications are not accepted . Each team must: Consist of 3–5 members Bring together diverse and complementary skills such as: Technical expertise Project management Financial planning Communication and stakeholder engagement Eligibility Criteria Individual Requirements Applicants must: Be 18–30 years old (as of March 2026) Be fluent in English (program delivery language) Commit 5–8 hours per week from May 2026 to September 2027 Have reliable internet access and a computer Show a strong interest in: Clean energy Sustainability Community development Demonstrate background or interest in: Climate Energy International cooperation Team Requirements Each team must: Include 3–5 members Have an identified or potential project site , such as: Schools Community centers Farms Non-profit facilities Demonstrate a mix of relevant skills across the team Program Timeline The Guided Projects Cohort 3 runs for 18 months , from: May 2026 to September 2027 Participants will progressively move through stages of project development, from planning to execution and final delivery. Real Impact: What Past Cohorts Have Achieved BeetBoxFarm Solar Project (Cohort 1) Installed a 5 kW solar system on a farm in Ottawa Generated projected returns of $35,000 Enabled reinvestment into sustainable farming practices Solar EV Team Project (Cohort 2) Developed a 2.18 kW solar installation Generated over 2,500 kWh annually Supported accessibility, inclusion, and cultural learning initiatives Alumni Experiences Participants consistently highlight the program’s value in building: Leadership and teamwork skills Technical expertise in solar energy systems Budgeting and stakeholder management experience Professional networks in the energy sector Many alumni have gone on to expand their projects, secure additional funding, and scale their impact within their communities. Application Deadline The deadline to apply is: April 26th, 2026 at 11:59 PM Pacific Time Late applications will not be considered, so ensure your team prepares and submits all required materials on time. How to Apply To apply, your team should: Form a group of 3–5 members Identify a potential project site Prepare a clear concept for your solar project Submit your application through the official Student Energy platform APPLY HERE About Student Energy Student Energy is a global youth-led organization working with over 50,000 young people across 120+ countries. The organization focuses on empowering the next generation of leaders to accelerate the transition to a sustainable and equitable energy future. Final Thoughts The Guided Projects Cohort 3 is more than just a program—it is a launchpad for young leaders passionate about clean energy and sustainability. By combining technical training, mentorship, funding, and real-world implementation, it provides a rare opportunity to build impactful solutions while gaining valuable career experience. If your team is ready to lead change and power the future, this is your moment. For more information about this opportunity, visit here Discover more Global Fellowship Opportunities on OFY here The post Join Student Energy Guided Projects Cohort 3 (Solar Stream): Build Real Solar Solutions and Transform Your Community appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Queer Forced Displacement Initiative Crisis Response Fund 2026: A Lifeline for LGBTQ+ Communities in Crisis
summary: The Queer Forced Displacement Initiative Crisis Response Fund is a vital global funding opportunity designed to address urgent humanitarian gaps affecting LGBTQ+ individuals and communities experiencing displacement. In a world where crisis situations ranging from conflict and persecution to socio-political instability continue to disproportionately impact LGBTQ+ populations, this fund offers a timely and flexible response mechanism to empower grassroots actors working on the frontlines. This initiative recognizes that local organizations and individuals are often the first responders in times of crisis, yet they frequently lack access to rapid and adaptable funding. The Crisis Response Fund bridges this gap by providing micro-grants that enable immediate, life-saving interventions while strengthening long-term resilience for LGBTQ+ communities. About the Crisis Response Fund The Crisis Response Fund is part of a broader effort to support LGBTQ+ people who are forcibly displaced or at risk of displacement. It focuses on funding grassroots, community-led responses that directly address the root causes and consequences of displacement. The fund prioritizes accessibility and flexibility, ensuring that even small or unregistered organizations can apply and receive support. Through this initiative, applicants can access one-time micro-funding of up to CAD $15,000. While the funding is not designed for long-term or recurring support, it plays a critical role in enabling rapid interventions during emergencies. Core Funding Streams To ensure targeted and effective interventions, the Crisis Response Fund operates through three key funding streams: 1. Crisis Emergency Response Services Grant This stream supports immediate, on-the-ground services for LGBTQ+ individuals facing displacement. Projects may include emergency shelter, access to healthcare, legal aid, psychosocial support, and protection services. 2. Crisis Capacity Building Grant This stream focuses on strengthening the operational and strategic capacity of grassroots organizations. It supports training, systems development, and resource-building efforts that enhance an organization’s ability to respond to crises effectively. 3. Crisis Advocacy Grant This stream funds advocacy initiatives aimed at addressing systemic issues that contribute to displacement. Projects may include policy engagement, awareness campaigns, and coalition-building efforts to improve conditions for LGBTQ+ communities. Funding Priorities and Considerations The Crisis Response Fund places a strong emphasis on projects that directly benefit LGBTQ+ individuals who are displaced or at high risk of displacement. Applicants are encouraged to design proposals that prioritize core program activities over administrative expenses. Key considerations include: Budget Currency Requirement: All budgets must be submitted in Canadian Dollars (CAD). Applications submitted in other currencies, such as USD, will not be reviewed. Operational Cost Limit: Operational or administrative costs should not exceed 20% of the total budget. Projects with lower operational costs are given higher priority. Clear Budget Alignment: Applicants must ensure that all proposed activities are clearly linked to the budget, with detailed justifications provided for each expense. Focus on Impact: Proposals should demonstrate tangible outcomes, particularly in improving safety, access to services, and resilience for LGBTQ+ individuals. Eligibility and Application Guidance The fund is open to small and medium-sized grassroots organizations, whether registered or unregistered, as well as individuals actively engaged in supporting LGBTQ+ communities in crisis. Applicants are strongly encouraged to review the official application criteria and use the eligibility checklist before submitting their proposals. Additionally, a comprehensive FAQ section is available to guide applicants through the process, helping them understand requirements, expectations, and best practices for a successful application. For further inquiries or assistance, applicants can reach out via email at: crisisfund@rainbowrailroad.org. Funding Rounds and Deadlines The Crisis Response Fund operates through region-specific funding rounds. While several rounds have already been completed, each targeting different geographic regions, the most recent updates are as follows: Round 1 (2024/25): Closed February 15, 2025 Round 2 (2025): Closed April 30, 2025 Round 3 (2025): Closed July 31, 2025 Round 4 (2025): Closed October 31, 2025 Round 5 (2026): Closed January 31, 2026 Round 6 (2026): Currently announced for the Middle East North Africa and Asia regions. Applicants from these regions should prepare for submission based on upcoming timelines and guidelines. Why This Opportunity Matters The Crisis Response Fund is more than just financial support—it is a strategic investment in community resilience and human dignity. By empowering grassroots actors, the initiative ensures that LGBTQ+ individuals facing displacement are not left behind in humanitarian responses. In a landscape where funding is often limited and slow to mobilize, this fund stands out for its agility, inclusivity, and commitment to addressing urgent needs. It provides a platform for local voices to lead solutions, ensuring that interventions are culturally relevant, context-specific, and impactful. Deadline : Depends on the round APPLY HERE For more information on this opportunity, click here. For more global opportunities , click here . The post Queer Forced Displacement Initiative Crisis Response Fund 2026: A Lifeline for LGBTQ+ Communities in Crisis appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group 2026: Global Call for Indigenous Experts to Influence Environmental Policy
summary: The Global Environment Facility has officially opened applications for its Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group, offering a unique opportunity for Indigenous leaders and experts to contribute to global environmental governance. This prestigious advisory body plays a critical role in shaping policies, programs, and initiatives that directly affect Indigenous Peoples and local communities worldwide. Established in 2012, the Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group (IPAG) serves as a bridge between Indigenous communities and the Global Environment Facility Secretariat. Through this platform, Indigenous voices are not only heard but actively integrated into global decision-making processes related to biodiversity conservation, climate change, and sustainable development. About the Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group (IPAG) The Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group is composed of experienced Indigenous leaders and experts from different regions of the world. Members serve in a voluntary capacity, contributing their knowledge, perspectives, and lived experiences to ensure that environmental policies are inclusive, equitable, and culturally sensitive. The Global Environment Facility is currently seeking to appoint four new members to the advisory group, representing diverse geographical and demographic backgrounds. The available positions include: One expert from Latin America and the Caribbean One expert from Asia (inland regions) One expert from Africa One independent Indigenous youth expert These positions reflect the organization’s commitment to regional balance, inclusivity, and intergenerational representation. Deadline for Applications All interested applicants must submit their expressions of interest by April 24, 2026. Late applications are unlikely to be considered, so early submission is strongly encouraged. Applicants can access the application forms and terms of reference in multiple languages, including English, French, and Spanish, making the process accessible to a broader audience. Roles and Responsibilities of IPAG Members Members of the Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group play a vital role in influencing global environmental strategies and ensuring that Indigenous perspectives are embedded in decision-making processes. Since its establishment, IPAG has contributed significantly to a wide range of initiatives and policy frameworks. One of the key responsibilities of IPAG members is contributing to the design and implementation of major conservation initiatives. This includes programs such as the Inclusive Conservation Initiative and the Heart of Conservation Initiative, which focus on empowering Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities to lead biodiversity conservation efforts and manage natural resources effectively. In addition, IPAG members provide valuable input into critical policy documents, including environmental and social safeguard frameworks. Their contributions have helped shape minimum standards specifically designed to protect the rights and interests of Indigenous Peoples within global environmental programs. IPAG also plays an active role in international advocacy. Members participate in major global forums such as the Convention on Biological Diversity, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, and the World Conservation Congress. Through these platforms, they promote awareness of Indigenous issues and strengthen engagement between Indigenous communities and global institutions. Another key function of IPAG is advisory support. Members provide guidance to the Global Environment Facility’s leadership, including the CEO and Secretariat, on matters relating to Indigenous Peoples. This includes advising on funding mechanisms, monitoring and evaluation frameworks, and strategies to enhance Indigenous participation in environmental projects. Furthermore, IPAG members engage with various international agencies through collaborative platforms such as the United Nations Inter-Agency Support Group on Indigenous Peoples. This collaboration facilitates knowledge sharing, improves access to financing, and strengthens program implementation. Why You Should Apply Joining the Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group offers a rare opportunity to influence global environmental policy at the highest level. It allows Indigenous experts to bring their perspectives into international discussions, ensuring that policies are grounded in community realities and traditional knowledge systems. Members gain exposure to global networks, collaborate with leading organizations, and contribute to impactful initiatives that shape the future of environmental sustainability. This role is particularly valuable for individuals who are passionate about advocacy, policy development, and community empowerment. Additionally, serving on IPAG enhances professional credibility and provides a platform for leadership in global environmental governance. Current Composition of IPAG The advisory group currently includes respected leaders and professionals from across the globe, representing various regions and expertise areas. Their collective work continues to strengthen Indigenous participation in global environmental decision-making processes. How to Apply Interested candidates should complete the application form and review the terms of reference carefully before submission. Applications are available in English, French, and Spanish to ensure inclusivity and accessibility. To begin your application, visit the official Global Environment Facility website and navigate to the Indigenous Peoples Advisory Group application page. Early preparation is recommended to ensure a strong application that clearly highlights your experience, leadership, and commitment to Indigenous advocacy and environmental sustainability. Deadline : 24th April,2026 APPLY HERE For more information on this opportunity, click here. For more global opportunities, click here . title: Decolonizing Humanities Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Opportunity for African and Southeast Asian Scholars to Reimagine Global Humanities
summary: The Decolonizing Humanities Fellowship 2026 presents a compelling and transformative opportunity for mid-career scholars from Africa and Southeast Asia (excluding Indonesia) who are committed to challenging entrenched colonial legacies in academic knowledge production. Hosted by the Department of Anthropology at Universitas Indonesia (UI) and supported by the Gerda Henkel Foundation, this fellowship is strategically designed to shift the center of gravity in global humanities discourse by amplifying Global South perspectives. In a world where Eurocentric frameworks continue to dominate scholarly narratives, this initiative directly addresses structural inequities that limit access to academic resources, collaboration, and influence for scholars in the Global South. By prioritizing South-to-South engagement, the fellowship fosters a new intellectual ecosystem grounded in critical, decolonial thinking and collaborative knowledge creation. About the Fellowship The program invites selected fellows to spend between 6 to 12 months at Universitas Indonesia, one of Southeast Asia’s leading academic institutions. Positioned at the crossroads of Asia and the Pacific, UI provides an intellectually rich environment for interdisciplinary collaboration, research innovation, and academic exchange. The fellowship is specifically tailored for scholars eager to experiment with new conceptual frameworks, collaborate across regions, and produce innovative academic outputs that challenge dominant narratives in the humanities. Core Objectives of the Fellowship The Decolonizing Humanities Fellowship is structured around several strategic objectives: Advancing humanities research rooted in decolonial perspectives from the Global South Facilitating long-term academic partnerships between African and Southeast Asian institutions Supporting the development of impactful academic publications and teaching innovations Strengthening institutional collaboration and teaching capacity at Universitas Indonesia What Fellows Will Do During the fellowship period, participants will be actively engaged in a variety of academic and collaborative activities designed to maximize impact: 1. Academic Colloquiums Fellows will deliver two major colloquiums: A closed-door session in the first month to refine research ideas with faculty and peers A public colloquium toward the end of the fellowship to present findings and stimulate broader academic discourse 2. Working Paper Development Each fellow is expected to produce at least one high-quality working paper aligned with their research focus, contributing to global conversations on decolonial scholarship. 3. Teaching and Academic Engagement Fellows will integrate into existing courses within the host department, offering seminars, workshops, and guest lectures. This engagement allows for direct interaction with students and faculty while fostering dynamic academic debates. 4. Collaborative Research Projects Participants will work in interdisciplinary teams to design and develop research initiatives that challenge colonial epistemologies and promote innovative methodologies. 5. Knowledge Dissemination Fellows are encouraged to share their research through academic journals, digital platforms, and public events, ensuring accessibility and broader societal impact. Fellowship Benefits and Financial Support The fellowship offers a comprehensive support package to ensure scholars can focus fully on their research and collaboration: Monthly stipend to cover living expenses in Indonesia Coverage of visa fees and research permits Reimbursement for international travel (up to a specified limit) Funding for research materials and supplies Family support, including: Two return trips for family visits Financial grants for children under 18 This holistic support structure ensures inclusivity and accessibility, particularly for scholars with family responsibilities. Expected Outcomes The fellowship is designed to produce tangible and long-lasting impact, including: Support for up to two fellows annually Publication of scholarly articles and development of teaching modules Organization of public lectures, workshops, and open-access knowledge platforms Strengthened academic networks across Africa and Southeast Asia Enhanced institutional capacity at Universitas Indonesia for global collaboration Application Process Interested applicants must submit an expression of interest via email. The application should clearly demonstrate academic excellence, a commitment to decolonial scholarship, and a willingness to engage in cross-regional collaboration. Subject Line: Application – Decolonizing Humanities Fellowship – Full Name Required Documents Applicants must submit the following in a single email: Curriculum Vitae (CV) A detailed academic CV outlining institutional affiliation, research experience, and publications. Research Proposal (Maximum 5 Pages) The proposal should include: Research objectives and key questions Conceptual and methodological approach Expected outputs such as publications or teaching modules Writing Sample (Optional but Encouraged) A recent publication or manuscript relevant to the proposed research area. Selection Process Applications will be evaluated by a dedicated selection committee based on: Academic merit Alignment with the fellowship’s decolonial agenda Potential for meaningful collaboration Shortlisted candidates may be contacted for further discussions, and successful applicants will be notified via email. Deadline All applications must be submitted by 30 April 2026 at 23:59 (GMT+7). APPLY HERE For more information on this opportunity , click here For more global opportunities , click here. The post Decolonizing Humanities Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Opportunity for African and Southeast Asian Scholars to Reimagine Global Humanities appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Apply Now: ul Haq Early Career Scholarships 2026 for PhD Students and Researchers (Global Opportunity)
summary: The Human Development and Capability Association (HDCA), in partnership with the United Nations Development Programme’s Human Development Report Office (UNDP/HDRO), is now accepting applications for the prestigious ul Haq Early Career Scholarships 2026 . This competitive mentorship programme is designed for outstanding early-career researchers committed to advancing the field of human development and capabilities. Successful applicants will join a global network of scholars and gain expert mentorship to support academic publishing and career growth. About Mahbub ul Haq The scholarship is named after Mahbub ul Haq (1934–1998) , a pioneering economist and architect of the Human Development Index (HDI) . His work revolutionized global development thinking by shifting the focus from economic growth to people-centered development. Through his leadership, the UNDP Human Development Reports became a cornerstone of international policy discourse. About the ul Haq Early Career Scholarships The ul Haq Scholarships aim to nurture the next generation of leaders in human development research. Selected scholars will contribute to knowledge creation, policy engagement, and global dialogue within and beyond the HDCA community. Start Date: Must commence by 1 September 2026 Duration: Up to 24 months Selection Process: Competitive, with possible virtual interviews Scholarship Benefits Recipients of the ul Haq Early Career Scholarships will enjoy a wide range of academic and professional benefits, including: Recognition as an ul Haq Scholar by HDCA and UNDP/HDRO Free HDCA membership throughout the scholarship period Personalized mentorship from leading experts in human development Support to publish in the Journal of Human Development and Capabilities (JHDC) Opportunity to present research in a global HDCA webinar Access to a global network of academics, practitioners, and policymakers Conference Support (Bursary Opportunity) Scholars may also apply for a conference bursary to attend an in-person HDCA global conference. The bursary may cover: Conference registration fees Travel expenses Accommodation Bursary awards are based on financial need, quality of submitted research abstracts, and active engagement in the mentorship programme. Eligibility Criteria To be eligible, applicants must: Be enrolled in a PhD programme in any discipline, or Be an early-career researcher within 5 years of completing a PhD Submit a writing sample (5,000–8,000 words) such as: Journal article Book chapter Thesis excerpt Special Track For 2026, applications are open under two tracks: General Track Lusophone Track (priority given to applicants from, working in, or writing in Portuguese within Lusophone countries) Selection will also consider disciplinary diversity, geographic representation, and inclusion factors . Scholar Responsibilities During the programme, selected scholars are expected to: Join and actively participate in HDCA thematic and regional groups Maintain active HDCA membership Present at least one HDRO–HDCA joint webinar Develop and submit a research paper to the Journal of Human Development and Capabilities , with guidance from an assigned mentor Application Timeline Application Deadline: 15 May 2026 Shortlisting (if interviews required): Mid-June 2026 Final Results: End of July 2026 All applications will be reviewed by a selection committee comprising representatives from HDCA and HDRO . Why You Should Apply The ul Haq Early Career Scholarship is more than funding—it is a career-shaping opportunity . With expert mentorship, global exposure, and strong publication support, it offers a powerful platform for emerging researchers to make meaningful contributions to human development scholarship. How to Apply Interested candidates should prepare their application materials, including a strong academic writing sample, and submit before the deadline. APPLY HERE Discover similar opportunities Here. title: Blue Economy Scholarship 2026: Fully Funded UBC Opportunity for East African Professionals (Apply Before April 30)
summary: The Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), in partnership with the globally renowned University of British Columbia (UBC), has launched an exciting opportunity for professionals in East Africa through the Blue Grant Scholarship under the BE-Prosper Project . This highly competitive and impactful scholarship is designed to build regional expertise in the blue economy , focusing on sustainable ocean management, fisheries, and marine governance across the IGAD and Horn of Africa region. If you are passionate about marine resources, sustainability, and regional development , this opportunity could shape your academic and leadership journey at one of the world’s top universities. Application Deadline The deadline to submit your screening application is April 30, 2026 . About the Blue Grant Scholarship (BE-Prosper Project) The Blue Grant Scholarship is part of the Building Blue Economy Resilience and Prosperity in IGAD Horn of Africa (BE-Prosper) initiative. This program aims to: Strengthen regional capacity in blue economy leadership Enhance knowledge in fisheries value chains and management Improve coastal governance and marine spatial planning Promote sustainable ocean and marine resource management This initiative is especially critical for countries in the Horn of Africa, where marine and coastal resources are key drivers of economic growth and environmental sustainability. Why Study at the University of British Columbia (UBC)? The University of British Columbia is globally recognized for excellence in: Fisheries economics and management Environmental sustainability Marine and ocean sciences As a scholar, you will gain: Access to world-class research facilities Opportunities to collaborate with leading global experts Exposure to cutting-edge solutions in blue economy development Program Structure: Two-Phase Application Process The application process is carefully structured to ensure the selection of the most qualified candidates. Phase 1: IGAD Screening Applicants must first undergo a screening process conducted by IGAD. Phase 2: UBC Graduate Admission Successful candidates from the IGAD screening will proceed to apply for admission to UBC, where final selection and scholarship placement occur. IGAD Screening Criteria To be considered, applicants should meet the following criteria: Interest in Blue Economy Demonstrated academic or professional interest in: Fisheries management Fish value chains Maritime connectivity and trade corridors Coastal and ocean resource management Educational Background A university degree in relevant disciplines such as: Marine and fisheries science Environmental economics Agriculture economics Maritime or civil engineering Human geography Veterinary science Community development Ocean policy or related fields Age Requirement Applicants must be between 25 and 40 years old Professional Experience Must be currently working in: Government or public sector Private sector NGOs or civil society organizations International or intergovernmental institutions Regional Eligibility Must be from: South Sudan Somalia Djibouti Motivation and Impact Strong commitment to: Sustainable fisheries Responsible marine resource use Blue economy development in the region Collaboration Interest Willingness to: Work with IGAD and UBC experts Contribute to solving regional challenges How to Apply for IGAD Screening Applicants are required to submit the following documents: Letter of Interest (LoI) addressing all screening criteria Updated Curriculum Vitae (CV) Copy of university degree Submission Details Send your application via email to: sagal.isman@igad.int Copy: tegueste.shimelis@igad.int For inquiries, contact: eshete.dejen@igad.int UBC Graduate Application Requirements Candidates who pass the IGAD screening will proceed to the next stage at UBC. The graduate application typically includes: Completed application form with fees Reference letters Statement of interest English language test (if applicable) Criminal record check Identification of a thesis supervisor Proof of citizenship For full application details, visit the UBC graduate admissions portal. Key Benefits of the Blue Grant Scholarship Although specific funding details are not explicitly outlined, the scholarship is described as fully funded , implying support for: Tuition fees Academic training Research opportunities International exposure Additionally, scholars benefit from: Participation in a high-impact regional initiative Development of leadership skills in blue economy sectors Contribution to sustainable development in East Africa Why This Opportunity Matters The blue economy represents one of the fastest-growing sectors globally, with immense potential for: Job creation Food security Environmental sustainability Regional trade Through this scholarship, IGAD and UBC aim to build a network of skilled professionals who will: Drive innovation in marine industries Influence policy and governance Strengthen regional cooperation Click HERE to learn more about the Blue Grant Scholarship Final Tips for Applicants To stand out in your application: Clearly align your experience with blue economy priorities Demonstrate real-world impact in your work Show strong motivation for regional development Be specific about how the program will help you achieve your goals Discover more scholarship opportunities on OFY HERE The post Blue Economy Scholarship 2026: Fully Funded UBC Opportunity for East African Professionals (Apply Before April 30) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: JEP 2026 – Call for Applications
summary: Applications are now open for JEP 2026 , a program designed for scholars advancing research in education policy with a focus on racial justice. About the Program JEP (Junior Education Policy) brings together emerging researchers in a collaborative learning environment to strengthen their research agendas and impact. Participants will: Engage in community-based learning with peers Refine and develop their research focus Receive mentorship and guidance from experienced scholars Explore how research can drive equity and systemic change Who Should Apply This opportunity is ideal for: Current doctoral students in education policy Postdoctoral researchers working on education and racial justice Scholars interested in equity-focused research and policy impact Applicants should demonstrate: A strong interest in racial justice in education Commitment to advancing impactful research Willingness to engage in collaborative learning Key Details Program: JEP 2026 Eligibility: Doctoral students and post-docs in education policy Focus Area: Racial justice in education Application Deadline: June 1, 2026 Why This Opportunity Matters JEP provides a supportive space for emerging scholars to build meaningful, equity-driven research while connecting with a community committed to transforming education systems.
